summary: DHH recounts his brief switch from Winamp to Windows Media Player in Windows XP, initially impressed by its music library features. After growing frustrated with codec downloads phoning home to Microsoft servers, slow performance with large music collections, and frequent crashes, he switched back to Winamp. He appreciates Winamp's instant responsiveness and lack of Microsoft's pushy media recommendations, while hoping Winamp will add better library management features.
key_points: Windows Media Player's polished library features initially seemed superior to Winamp but couldn't overcome fundamental usability problems
Microsoft's sneaky server connections for codecs and embedded media recommendations felt intrusive
Performance matters more than features — instant response on an 8GB playlist beats a fancy but sluggish interface
Software that respects users by not pushing unwanted content creates a better experience
money_quotes: On the surface it's clever indexing of artists, genres, and albums seemed like enough to blow long-time favorite Winamp out of the water.
I finally grew decisively impatient with the sneaky codec trips to Microsoft's servers, the 5+ second delays when visiting a media library with 8 gigs of MP3s, and the frequent crashes.
So Windows Media Player is out and Winamp is back in -- and it feels good.
Having an instant response on pressing "Next Song" on my 8 gig playlist brings a lot of satisfaction.
Not having to be exposed to Microsoft's recommendations on music and movies everytime I want to hear a particular song is equally nice.
key_insight: Flashy features cannot compensate for poor performance, instability, and software that secretly serves its maker's interests over the user's.
